content.text: Event Storming is a collaborative modelling technique in which domain experts, engineers, and product owners map a system's behaviour as a timeline of named domain events on a shared wall (or virtual board). Introduced by Alberto Brandolini, it is highly effective for surfacing hidden complexity in user journeys. GenAI features introduce a category of events and actors that standard Event Storming notation does not capture well — model inference calls, non-deterministic output paths, vector retrieval, guardrail evaluations, and feedback loops that update the model itself. This document adapts the technique for GenAI feature discovery.

attrs.alt: Event Storming swimlane map for a GenAI feature: six swimlanes showing Domain Events (orange), Commands (green), Read Models (blue), External Systems (red), AI Decisions (purple), and Governance events (yellow)

content.text: Why Standard Event Storming Needs Adaptation for GenAI
In a standard Event Storming session, every sticky note is either a
(something that happened, past tense, orange), a
(data needed to issue the command, blue), or an
(a box the domain calls, pink). This palette maps well to deterministic CRUD systems.
GenAI features break two core Event Storming assumptions:

The same command issued twice produces different events. An LLM's 'ResponseGenerated' event carries a probability distribution, not a single outcome. Participants need a way to annotate events as non-deterministic hot-spots where re-runs may produce different outputs, different costs, and different safety profiles.

In a rule engine, you can trace why an event occurred. In an LLM, 'ContentFlagged' may result from training data, the system prompt, the user's prompt, retrieved context, or temperature setting — all interacting in ways that are not inspectable at event time. The model is simultaneously a 'Policy' (it makes decisions) and an 'External System' (you cannot look inside it).
Adapting Event Storming for GenAI means extending the sticky-note palette with two additional types and introducing a new swimlane for AI-internal processing.
Extended Notation for GenAI Event Storms
A decision made by a model — non-deterministic, probabilistic
A domain event whose content varies across runs
A point where output quality is assessed — online or offline
An event that flows back to update training data or prompts
Add two swimlanes to the standard layout:

Contains AI Decision stickies — inference calls, chain-of-thought steps, tool calls made by the agent. This swimlane makes model invocations explicit and visible to domain experts who may not know they are happening.

Contains events that must be recorded for compliance — token consumption, PII detection, content policy decisions, and model version at inference time. Making this swimlane explicit surfaces the audit trail requirements during discovery rather than post-launch.
Running a GenAI Event Storming Session
Product owner or domain expert (provides business events)
ML engineer (annotates AI Decision nodes and their uncertainty)
Backend engineer (identifies integration points and external systems)
Data engineer (identifies what data is produced, stored, or consumed)
Everyone writes domain events (orange) independently — no discussion, no ordering
Group moves events into rough chronological order, debate and merge duplicates
ML engineer marks which events are produced by model inference — adds purple AI Decision stickies
Add green commands and blue read models; identify what data each AI Decision needs
Causal chains from command to event
Red dots on events that are non-deterministic, expensive, or legally sensitive
What must be logged, retained, or reported? Add yellow audit events
Identify bounded contexts, open questions, and follow-up workshops

The most valuable part of the GenAI adaptation is the AI Decision annotation phase. Non-technical domain experts often do not know that a feature they see as 'the system responds' is actually 'an LLM running 3 tool calls and generating 800 tokens'. Making this explicit frequently reveals compliance gaps, latency expectations misalignments, and cost surprises that would otherwise surface only after launch.
Mapping Events to GenAI System Components
After the session, each sticky maps to a system component. This mapping drives the architecture and the data model.

# Domain events and their system component ownership
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class StickyType(str, Enum):
    DOMAIN_EVENT      = "domain_event"     # Orange
    COMMAND           = "command"          # Green
    READ_MODEL        = "read_model"       # Blue
    EXTERNAL_SYSTEM   = "external_system"  # Pink
    AI_DECISION       = "ai_decision"      # Purple
    EVAL_HOOK         = "eval_hook"        # Dark blue
    FEEDBACK_LOOP     = "feedback_loop"    # Grey

@dataclass
class EventStormSticky:
    name: str                        # e.g. "PromptSubmitted"
    sticky_type: StickyType
    swimlane: str                    # "user_interaction" | "ai_processing" | "external" | ...
    is_non_deterministic: bool = False
    is_legally_sensitive: bool = False
    produces_pii: bool = False
    must_be_logged: bool = False
    owning_service: Optional[str] = None  # Determined post-session

    def is_audit_required(self) -> bool:
        return self.is_legally_sensitive or self.produces_pii or self.must_be_logged

    def risk_flags(self) -> list[str]:
        flags = []
        if self.is_non_deterministic:  flags.append("NON_DETERMINISTIC")
        if self.is_legally_sensitive:  flags.append("LEGAL_SENSITIVE")
        if self.produces_pii:          flags.append("PII")
        if self.must_be_logged:        flags.append("MUST_LOG")
        return flags

# Example event map from a GenAI customer support feature
events = [
    EventStormSticky("PromptSubmitted",          StickyType.COMMAND,        "user_interaction"),
    EventStormSticky("PiiScrubbed",              StickyType.DOMAIN_EVENT,   "ai_processing",  produces_pii=True,  must_be_logged=True),
    EventStormSticky("VectorSearchExecuted",     StickyType.COMMAND,        "external_system"),
    EventStormSticky("ContextRetrieved",         StickyType.READ_MODEL,     "ai_processing"),
    EventStormSticky("ResponseGenerated",        StickyType.AI_DECISION,    "ai_processing",  is_non_deterministic=True),
    EventStormSticky("GuardrailEvaluated",       StickyType.AI_DECISION,    "ai_processing",  is_legally_sensitive=True, must_be_logged=True),
    EventStormSticky("ContentFlagged",           StickyType.DOMAIN_EVENT,   "governance",     must_be_logged=True),
    EventStormSticky("ResponseDelivered",        StickyType.DOMAIN_EVENT,   "user_interaction"),
    EventStormSticky("TokenCostRecorded",        StickyType.DOMAIN_EVENT,   "governance",     must_be_logged=True),
    EventStormSticky("FeedbackThumbsDown",       StickyType.FEEDBACK_LOOP,  "user_interaction"),
    EventStormSticky("FinetuneExampleQueued",    StickyType.FEEDBACK_LOOP,  "ai_processing"),
    EventStormSticky("EvalScoreRecorded",        StickyType.EVAL_HOOK,      "governance"),
]

audit_events = [e for e in events if e.is_audit_required()]
nd_events    = [e for e in events if e.is_non_deterministic]
print(f"Total events: {len(events)}")
print(f"Audit-required: {[e.name for e in audit_events]}")
print(f"Non-deterministic: {[e.name for e in nd_events]}")
From Event Storm to Feature Backlog
The output of the session drives three artefacts that feed directly into sprint planning:
Cluster the sticky notes into groups that can be owned by a single team or service. In a GenAI feature this typically produces:
Everything from PromptSubmitted through PiiScrubbed and ContextRetrieved — owned by the Application team.
AI Decision events (ResponseGenerated, GuardrailEvaluated) — owned by the ML Platform team.
EvalHook and FeedbackLoop events — owned by the ML Quality team.
All Governance swimlane events — owned by the Platform/Security team.

Every red-dot hotspot becomes a design spike. The spike must answer: 'How do we handle non-deterministic output in this part of the flow?' Common spike outputs are consistency policies (re-rank, retry with lower temperature, human-in-the-loop fallback) and evaluation harnesses.

Each domain event is a candidate for an event schema. Writing schema stubs during the session — while domain experts are in the room — prevents the common failure of engineering defining event schemas in isolation and missing the business concepts that matter.

# Schema stubs generated from event storm — fill in post-session
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Optional
import uuid

@dataclass
class PromptSubmittedEvent:
    event_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    timestamp: datetime = field(default_factory=datetime.utcnow)
    session_id: str = ""
    user_id_hash: str = ""       # PII: hash only, never plaintext
    raw_prompt_hash: str = ""    # Hash for dedup; raw prompt stored in PII vault
    channel: str = ""            # "web" | "mobile" | "api"
    feature_flag_variant: Optional[str] = None

@dataclass
class ResponseGeneratedEvent:
    event_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    timestamp: datetime = field(default_factory=datetime.utcnow)
    prompt_event_id: str = ""    # Correlates back to PromptSubmittedEvent
    model_id: str = ""           # e.g. "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-70B-Instruct"
    model_version: str = ""
    temperature: float = 0.7
    input_tokens: int = 0
    output_tokens: int = 0
    latency_ms: int = 0
    # Note: response_text NOT stored here — stored in content vault with retention policy
    content_vault_ref: Optional[str] = None

@dataclass
class GuardrailEvaluatedEvent:
    event_id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    timestamp: datetime = field(default_factory=datetime.utcnow)
    response_event_id: str = ""
    guardrail_id: str = ""       # e.g. "llama-guard-2"
    passed: bool = True
    violation_category: Optional[str] = None  # e.g. "S6:Privacy" if failed
    latency_ms: int = 0
Common Discoveries from GenAI Event Storm Sessions
Practitioners running this adapted technique consistently surface the same categories of surprises:

Domain experts describing a 'respond instantly' requirement often mean 200–500 ms. A vector search + LLM inference chain in the naive implementation takes 1,500–4,000 ms. The event storm makes this timeline visible, triggering a design conversation about streaming, caching, or model size reduction before any code is written.

The Governance swimlane frequently contains events that nobody has claimed: 'who owns TokenCostRecorded? Who is responsible for ContentFlagged going to the compliance dashboard?' These gaps in ownership are discovered in 3 hours instead of in a production incident.

FeedbackLoop stickies (thumbs-down, correction submitted, escalation triggered) often have no downstream handler. The event exists in the user's mental model but not in the engineering plan. Surfacing this during discovery drives earlier investment in evaluation infrastructure.

Teams often annotate the model output as non-deterministic but miss that the vector search results are also sensitive to embedding model version and index staleness. The red-dot hotspot pass frequently produces a richer risk map than teams expect.

When the GenAI feature involves an agentic loop — an LLM that calls tools, reflects on intermediate results, and decides what to do next — the linear event timeline of standard Event Storming breaks down. Agentic flows are not linear; they branch, retry, and can run for minutes or hours. The Event Storming adaptation for agentic systems uses a
with explicit entry and exit conditions on the timeline.
Loop Start sticky (vertical orange bar on the left):
Marks the start of an agent's reasoning cycle. Label it with the agent's name and the trigger condition.
Tool Call stickies (pink, distinct from External System):
Each tool the agent may call is a separate sticky in the AI Processing swimlane, showing the tool name, expected input, and possible failure modes.
The LLM's intermediate reasoning output — not surfaced to the user but visible in logs. This sticky surfaces the need for chain-of-thought logging, which is frequently missed.

The conditions under which the agent stops the loop — maximum iterations reached, confidence threshold met, explicit STOP tool call, or user interruption. Making exit conditions explicit during discovery prevents infinite-loop bugs and surfaces the need for timeout and cost-cap enforcement.

# Events in an agentic research assistant flow
# (modelled from an Event Storming session output)

AGENTIC_RESEARCH_FLOW = [
    # --- Loop Entry ---
    {"name": "ResearchTaskReceived",    "type": "domain_event",   "swimlane": "user_interaction"},
    {"name": "AgentLoopStarted",        "type": "domain_event",   "swimlane": "ai_processing",   "loop_marker": "entry"},
    # --- Iteration 1 ---
    {"name": "QueryDecomposed",         "type": "ai_decision",    "swimlane": "ai_processing",   "non_deterministic": True},
    {"name": "WebSearchCalled",         "type": "tool_call",      "swimlane": "external_system"},
    {"name": "SearchResultsRetrieved",  "type": "read_model",     "swimlane": "external_system"},
    {"name": "ReflectionGenerated",     "type": "ai_decision",    "swimlane": "ai_processing",   "reflection": True},
    # --- Iteration 2 ---
    {"name": "FollowUpQueryFormed",     "type": "ai_decision",    "swimlane": "ai_processing",   "non_deterministic": True},
    {"name": "CitationCheckerCalled",   "type": "tool_call",      "swimlane": "external_system"},
    {"name": "SourcesVerified",         "type": "domain_event",   "swimlane": "external_system"},
    # --- Exit ---
    {"name": "SufficientEvidenceReached","type": "domain_event",  "swimlane": "ai_processing",   "loop_marker": "exit"},
    {"name": "AgentLoopTerminated",     "type": "domain_event",   "swimlane": "ai_processing"},
    # --- Post-loop ---
    {"name": "DraftReportGenerated",    "type": "ai_decision",    "swimlane": "ai_processing",   "non_deterministic": True},
    {"name": "TotalTokenCostLogged",    "type": "domain_event",   "swimlane": "governance",      "must_log": True},
    {"name": "IterationCountLogged",    "type": "domain_event",   "swimlane": "governance",      "must_log": True},
    {"name": "ReportDelivered",         "type": "domain_event",   "swimlane": "user_interaction"},
]

# Post-session: count governance events and identify cost-control requirements
governance_events = [e for e in AGENTIC_RESEARCH_FLOW if e.get("swimlane") == "governance"]
tool_calls        = [e for e in AGENTIC_RESEARCH_FLOW if e.get("type") == "tool_call"]
exit_conditions   = [e for e in AGENTIC_RESEARCH_FLOW if e.get("loop_marker") == "exit"]

print(f"Tool calls requiring error handling: {[e['name'] for e in tool_calls]}")
print(f"Exit conditions to implement: {[e['name'] for e in exit_conditions]}")
print(f"Governance events (logging required): {[e['name'] for e in governance_events]}")
# Tool calls requiring error handling: ['WebSearchCalled', 'CitationCheckerCalled']
# Exit conditions to implement: ['SufficientEvidenceReached']
# Governance events (logging required): ['TotalTokenCostLogged', 'IterationCountLogged']

The exit condition 'SufficientEvidenceReached' is an AI Decision — the agent decides when it has enough evidence. This is non-deterministic and cost-sensitive. The design spike must answer: what is the maximum iteration count? What happens when the token budget is exhausted mid-loop? Who receives the partial result?
Remote Event Storming with Digital Tools

In distributed teams, Event Storming moves from a physical wall to a digital whiteboard (Miro, FigJam, Mural, or MIRO). The GenAI-adapted palette maps straightforwardly to coloured sticky note templates. A few practices specific to the remote format:

Create a Miro board with five swimlane rows pre-labelled and a sticky note legend in the top-right corner. Provide the extended colour palette as a set of Miro sticky-note presets in the team's shared library so participants don't need to debate colours during the session.
Lock the swimlane labels and legend before the session starts — they should not be accidentally moved.
Create a 'Parking Lot' frame outside the main timeline for questions, assumptions, and contested events. In GenAI sessions the parking lot fills quickly with uncertainty about model behaviour.
Use Miro's voting stickers to dot-vote on hotspots during the marking phase — this scales better than physical red dots in a large remote group.
Export the board as a structured JSON or CSV after the session so the events can be imported into a backlog tool (Jira, Linear) without manual retyping.
Digital Event Export for Backlog Import

# Export Event Storming stickies to Jira-importable CSV
import csv, io
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class MiroSticky:
    text: str
    colour: str              # "orange" | "green" | "blue" | "pink" | "purple" | "grey"
    swimlane: str
    x: float                 # Position on Miro canvas
    y: float
    tags: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

def classify_sticky(s: MiroSticky) -> dict:
    COLOUR_MAP = {
        "orange": ("Domain Event", "story"),
        "green":  ("Command",      "task"),
        "blue":   ("Read Model",   "subtask"),
        "pink":   ("External System", "epic"),
        "purple": ("AI Decision",  "story"),
        "grey":   ("Feedback Loop","task"),
    }
    etype, issue_type = COLOUR_MAP.get(s.colour, ("Unknown", "story"))
    return {
        "summary":     s.text,
        "issuetype":   issue_type,
        "labels":      f"{etype},EventStorm,{s.swimlane}," + ",".join(s.tags),
        "description": f"Event Storming: {etype} in swimlane '{s.swimlane}'. Position: ({s.x:.0f}, {s.y:.0f}).",
        "priority":    "High" if "hotspot" in s.tags else "Medium",
    }

def export_to_csv(stickies: list[MiroSticky]) -> str:
    buf = io.StringIO()
    fieldnames = ["summary", "issuetype", "labels", "description", "priority"]
    writer = csv.DictWriter(buf, fieldnames=fieldnames)
    writer.writeheader()
    for s in stickies:
        writer.writerow(classify_sticky(s))
    return buf.getvalue()
Connecting the Event Storm to Acceptance Criteria

The most common failure mode after an Event Storming session is that the output sits on the whiteboard and engineers write acceptance criteria independently, missing the nuances surfaced during the session. A lightweight mapping convention connects the two artefacts directly.
in the event storm, write at least three acceptance criteria:
Given a standard input, the AI Decision produces an output in the expected format within the agreed latency budget.
Running the same input 10 times, at least N% of responses meet the correctness criterion as measured by the agreed evaluation harness.
When the model is unavailable / returns a malformed output / violates the guardrail, the system falls back to the defined fallback behaviour (error message, rule-based default, human escalation).
, write an additional acceptance criterion specifying the consistency policy: retry logic, re-ranking strategy, or human review threshold.
, write an acceptance criterion specifying the retention period, access controls, and the name of the monitoring dashboard or SIEM alert that will verify the event is being logged.

Event Storming adapted for GenAI is one of the highest-return discovery investments a product team can make. The three-hour session cost is paid back in avoided rework when PII handling, audit requirements, latency budgets, and feedback loops are discovered during design rather than during QA or post-launch. Key adaptations to the standard technique:
make model inference explicit and visible to non-technical participants.
surface the risk map for evaluation and consistency planning.
converts compliance requirements from post-launch retrofits into first-class design inputs.
— bounded context map, hotspot backlog, and event schema stubs — provide direct inputs to sprint planning without requiring a separate translation step.
